chapters.title: How processed food took over the kitchen
chapters.text:
Home-cooked meals used to be the rule, but in recent years the food industry has flooded the market with heavily processed products. What makes a meal appealing comes down to many things, including sweet, sour, spicy, and other flavors the taste buds can pick up. Research has shown that sweetness is the one flavor we notice more than any other.

We pick up sweetness more than any other flavor, so we are built to crave sweet dishes and snacks. Our sweet tooth is not really a tooth, by the way. It is our tongue, parts of the roof of our mouth, and even parts of the stomach that do the sensing. That fact is not new to the big food companies that sell us processed food.

Never underestimate how much sugar is hiding in what you eat.

Once the food companies knew this, they began loading their products with extra sugar and other additives for the rest of us.

In the 1950s, television had just arrived, and more women were starting jobs that left them less time to cook. The timing was perfect for the food industry. Catchy commercials and products designed to wake up the sweet tooth brought in a growing customer base. These new, "convenient" foods were not enough to push home cooking aside, though, mostly because home economics teachers were still teaching kids how to cook.

No matter how long it takes to prepare, home-cooked food is usually the better choice.

There was no need to rely on processed food when kids could cook for themselves. The fix was to bring home economics teachers on board and have them endorse processed meals as the better option. It worked almost overnight. Support poured in, and home cooking lost ground, thanks to the sugar, fat, and salt baked into these new products. That is a quick history lesson. In the chapters ahead, we will dig into the food industry, see its tricks, and learn how you can protect yourself from unhealthy eating.

chapters.title: Sugar is everywhere in processed food
chapters.text:
Back when humans were just starting to climb the food chain, we craved sugary food in large amounts. Not necessarily sweet candy, but anything with sugars that the body could turn into energy. Fruits and starchy foods carry plenty of sugar and gave early humans the fuel to run from predators or to fight them off.

It is no longer wise to load up on sugary food, because too much can lead to serious health problems. We may want sugar all the time, but our bodies have a built-in ceiling for how much we can handle. Even if we cannot stop ourselves, there is a point where we really cannot take more. The processed food companies, of course, know this useful fact.

We have to learn to tame our craving for sugary food to avoid health trouble.

Not every company pays attention to that ceiling. In fact, many of them choose to ignore it and pile on as much sugar as they can. Most people do not realize how much sugar is hiding in the processed food and drinks they consume, which is exactly what makes them reach for more. The average American eats around 22 teaspoons of sugar a day, and more than 70 percent of that comes from processed food alone.

We eat these products without thinking about our health because they taste good, and we slowly damage our bodies. The damage does not stop at products that list sugar on the label, either. Plenty of foods do not need sugar but have it added anyway, so even a plain bowl of pasta can come with extra sugar.

Tasty or not, processed meals are best avoided.

Pasta sauce is one example. After tomatoes, the main ingredient is often sugar. These pairings are odd and unfair, because sugar has no business being in a savory dish.

If sugar is the stimulant of the processed food world, hitting the brain fast and hard, then fat is the slow operator, sliding in quietly with a powerful effect of its own.

The average can of tomato paste carries at least 12 grams of sugar, which is around six sugar cubes.

chapters.title: People kept eating the same harmful food
chapters.text:
By the 1970s, it was becoming clear that the steady rise in sugar and processed food was doing real damage to public health, and yet people kept buying the same products.

The boom in sweet food led to a sharp jump in cavities among children. Looking closer, researchers found an unsettling pattern driven by the most powerful ad tool of the era, the television. Kids were watching commercials for sweet breakfast cereals, and later studies showed that those cereals were loaded with sugar, which fed the rise in childhood cavities. Sugar brought other health risks too, including a higher chance of diabetes. While children dealt with their teeth, adults were facing life-threatening complications from the same disease. Throughout the 1970s, Americans were buried under a wave of illness that traced back to processed food and quick meals.

It is wise to be skeptical of what you see on TV, because those foods may look good but are not good for you.

Strangely, none of this slowed the demand. Even as people learned that the food was harming them, they kept buying the well-packaged products that were making them sick.

Cutting back on sugar is the kindest thing you can do for your health.

In the 1980s, the public conversation about processed food got louder, but the sales kept climbing. Coca-Cola and other giants posted steady profit growth for decades, and by the end of the 1990s the average American was dealing with obesity, diabetes, and heart disease in large numbers.

That is how the sweetened breakfast was born, along with a core industry trick: swap out the problem ingredient for another one that the public is not yet worried about.

chapters.title: Fat is just as dangerous as sugar
chapters.text:
Sugar is the first of the three big troublemakers in the food industry, and it is plenty powerful on its own, but fat is in a league of its own. We are wired to crave sugar and anything that tastes like it. Processed food companies know this, so they pour extra sugar into their products and reap the rewards.

Here is the catch. To do real damage, sugar has to be added in large amounts, but the sugar in processed food only accounts for about half the calories that fat would in the same space. Gram for gram, fat has roughly twice the calories of sugar. That is a lot of extra calories, especially when we cannot taste when fat is too much.

Watching for sugar matters, but watching for fat matters just as much.

Our bodies have a built-in stop sign for sugar, so at least there is a natural brake. Fat has no such brake. We cannot taste it when it sits next to sugar, and we cannot tell when we have had too much. That gap is what lets processed food companies load their products with fat and still get away with it. The result is that the average American blows past the daily fat limit by about 50 percent, and obesity is the predictable outcome. Sugar and fat work as a team to keep us reaching for more. Fat helps products last longer on the shelf, which gives us more time to buy them, and sugar keeps us coming back for another bite.

Processed food is full of fat that helps it sit on the shelf, but that same fat is hard on your heart, so fresh food is the better pick.

The risks of all that fat and the calories that come with it should be obvious, but the line from fat to heart disease and type 2 diabetes is a straight one. The body can only burn through so many calories when we are sitting still.

chapters.title: Some "healthy" foods are not healthy at all
chapters.text:
We know the basic food groups and the nutrients they provide, so we should be able to tell what to eat and what to skip. If that is true, then why do people who seem to eat well still put on extra weight and end up in the same shape as those who eat junk?

Two foods are at the center of that puzzle, milk and cheese. Cheese is already high in fat from the start, and processed cheese has even more. Two-thirds of the calories in cheese come from fat. The way cheese is made helps explain the heavy fat content.

Fat is a real danger, and cheese is loaded with it, so it is wise to cut back.

In the 1950s, demand for milk was growing, and the government wanted to keep milk affordable. They paid farmers a subsidy to keep production high, and the price stayed low. With so much milk on the market, health-conscious shoppers started asking for low-fat dairy. That was easy to arrange, but it left producers with a new problem. Where should all the skimmed milk fat go?

Since the government was paying for the milk and every product that came with it, the farmers decided to turn the extra fat into cheese and sell it to shoppers. This "cheese" was full of saturated fat, which was exactly what people were trying to avoid, but they happily ate it anyway, and cheese consumption climbed.

When the government tried to bring sugar levels down, consumers pushed back and asked for the old recipes. With cheese, the government kept funding the single biggest threat to public health, and most people did not even notice.

Regulators do their best, but you have to take responsibility for what you put in your body.

Once again, most people ate whatever was on the shelf without checking. Studies show that cheese consumption in America has roughly tripled since 1970.

chapters.title: Salt is the third hidden danger
chapters.text:
Salt often gets ignored because it does not carry the same calorie load as sugar and fat. It will not pile up as body fat, but it is not harmless either. The body needs a small amount of sodium for important functions, and salt is the main way we get it.

Too much sodium in the blood makes the blood thinner and pushes it through the body faster. That change can grow into a much more serious problem called hypertension. This information was not widely known in earlier decades, so salt use climbed without anyone realizing the cost. By the 1980s, one in four Americans had hypertension.

Sodium is fine in small amounts, but salt intake has to be kept in check to prevent hypertension.

The early numbers were fuzzy, but research eventually pointed straight at salt. The average American eats 10 to 20 times more salt than the body needs in a day. That level of excess was hard to ignore, and the government stepped in to regulate the amount of salt in packaged food.

The real culprit, though, was processed food. The average canned or packaged meal often contains at least twice the safe daily limit of salt. That sounds extreme, but the food companies were not trying to poison anyone. So why was there so much salt?

One guess is that food tastes better with salt, so the more, the better. That is part of the story, but it does not explain the full amount. The bigger reason is the same as for fat, which is shelf life. Salt helps products sit on the shelf for longer, which means more sales, and the cost is paid by the people who eat them.

Salt is easy to overlook, and that is exactly why processed food and salty snacks are best kept off the menu.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
It is so easy to pop a pre-made meal into the microwave and have dinner ready in a few minutes, the original meaning of fast food. That promise was the lure, but the food came loaded with more than catchy ads. It came packed with three of the most powerful ingredients in the modern diet, sugar, salt, and fat. Most people had no idea of the harm, and they kept eating until the consequences showed up in the form of obesity and disease.

Studies show that many of the things we eat contain enough of these three ingredients to cover the daily needs of four or five adults. Consumers are often unaware of the risks they take with every bite. Even when regulators asked the food companies to cut back on sugar, fat, and salt, the public pushed back and asked for the old recipes. Our bodies are wired to want more of these ingredients, which is a kind of built-in sabotage. The good news is that we know far more about this today, and that knowledge can help people take better care of themselves.

The idea that some foods act like drugs on the brain goes back about twenty years in the scientific world.

Try this

Watch out for your own health, because the food companies are watching out for their profits. Take responsibility for what you eat, and you stand a much better chance of staying well.

Did you make it to the end?
